title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

frames.caption: Initialize the window [lo, hi] = [0, 4]. The minimum (the rotation point) lies somewhere inside.
frames.note: A sorted array rotated at an unknown pivot is no longer globally sorted, but at least one half of any [lo, hi] window is still sorted — and that residual order is enough to binary-search. The minimum is exactly the rotation point (the one place where a bigger value is immediately followed by a smaller one). The closed window [lo, hi] is guaranteed to contain it, and each step shrinks the window while keeping that invariant true.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (4 - 0)/2 = 2. Compare nums[2] = 5 against nums[hi] = nums[4] = 2.
frames.note: Compute mid as lo + (hi - lo) / 2 to avoid the classic (lo + hi) overflow. The whole trick is the single comparison nums[mid] vs nums[hi]: comparing to the RIGHT end (not nums[lo]) localizes the rotation point cleanly, because nums[hi] always sits in the rotated/lower run once one exists, giving an unambiguous yes/no on which side the minimum lies.

frames.caption: nums[2] = 5 > nums[4] = 2: the min is to the RIGHT. Discard [lo..mid]; lo = mid + 1 = 3.
frames.note: nums[mid] > nums[hi] means mid is still on the higher, left-hand sorted run — the values dropped somewhere after mid. So the minimum (the pivot) is strictly to the right of mid. Set lo = mid + 1: this excludes mid (it cannot be the answer) and strictly shrinks the window, which is what guarantees termination.

frames.caption: mid = 3 + (4 - 3)/2 = 3. Compare nums[3] = 1 against nums[hi] = nums[4] = 2.
frames.note: Compute mid as lo + (hi - lo) / 2 to avoid the classic (lo + hi) overflow. The whole trick is the single comparison nums[mid] vs nums[hi]: comparing to the RIGHT end (not nums[lo]) localizes the rotation point cleanly, because nums[hi] always sits in the rotated/lower run once one exists, giving an unambiguous yes/no on which side the minimum lies.

frames.caption: nums[3] = 1 <= nums[3] = 1: the min is at mid or LEFT. Keep mid as a candidate; hi = mid = 3.
frames.note: nums[mid] <= nums[hi] means everything from mid to hi is already non-decreasing, so the minimum is at mid or to its left. Set hi = mid — NOT mid - 1 — because mid itself is still a candidate for the smallest value. Using a floored mid with hi = mid is safe here: lo can never equal mid while lo < hi, so the window always shrinks.

frames.caption: lo == hi == 3: the window is a single index. Return nums[3] = 1 — the minimum.
frames.note: When lo == hi the window has collapsed to a single index, and by the invariant that index is the minimum. We return nums[lo]. Each iteration halved the window, so this converges in O(log n) — the residual sortedness of one half is all we needed, no full sort or linear scan required.
